title: What is Linear Regression
content:
Linear Regression is a supervised machine learning algorithm used to model the relationship
between one or more independent variables (features) and a dependent variable (target).
Its objective is to learn a linear relationship that can predict continuous numerical values.
The model estimates coefficients that minimize the difference between predicted and observed
values. Linear regression is one of the oldest and most widely used statistical learning
techniques. It serves as the foundation for many advanced regression algorithms and is often
used as a baseline model. Typical applications include house price prediction, sales
forecasting, demand estimation, energy consumption prediction, and risk analysis.

title: Historical Background
content:
The method of least squares was introduced independently by Adrien-Marie Legendre in 1805
and Carl Friedrich Gauss later formalized its statistical interpretation. Linear regression
became a cornerstone of statistics before becoming one of the most important algorithms in
machine learning. Modern libraries such as scikit-learn provide efficient implementations,
making it practical for datasets containing thousands or millions of observations.

title: Real-world Applications
content:
Linear Regression is widely used whenever the prediction target is continuous. Common
applications include predicting house prices, estimating insurance costs, forecasting sales,
predicting temperatures, estimating fuel efficiency, demand forecasting, and financial trend
analysis. Although simple, the algorithm often provides a strong baseline against which more
complex models are compared.

title: Mathematical Model
content:
For simple linear regression the model is

y = b0 + b1*x

where b0 is the intercept and b1 is the slope.

For multiple linear regression,

y = b0 + b1*x1 + b2*x2 + ... + bn*xn

The coefficients represent the expected change in the target variable for a one-unit change
in the corresponding feature while keeping the remaining features fixed.

title: Cost Function
content:
The objective of training is to minimize the Mean Squared Error (MSE). The loss function
computes the average squared difference between actual and predicted values. Squaring the
errors penalizes large mistakes more heavily than small ones and results in a smooth,
differentiable optimization objective.

title: Ordinary Least Squares
content:
Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) estimates coefficients by minimizing the sum of squared
residuals. Under standard assumptions, OLS provides unbiased coefficient estimates.
The closed-form solution can be written using matrix algebra as
beta = (X^T X)^(-1) X^T y, provided X^T X is invertible.

title: Gradient Descent
content:
Instead of solving the normal equation directly, gradient descent iteratively updates
the model parameters. Starting from random or zero values, coefficients are repeatedly
adjusted in the direction that reduces the loss function. The learning rate controls the
step size. Too small a learning rate slows convergence, while too large a value can prevent
the algorithm from converging.

title: Model Assumptions
content:
Linear regression assumes a linear relationship between predictors and target, independent
observations, constant error variance (homoscedasticity), approximately normally distributed
residuals for inference, and low multicollinearity among predictors. Violating these
assumptions may reduce interpretability or predictive performance.

title: Evaluation Metrics
content:
Regression models are commonly evaluated using Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Mean Squared Error
(MSE), Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), and the coefficient of determination (R²). Lower MAE,
MSE, and RMSE indicate better predictive accuracy, while R² measures the proportion of target
variance explained by the model.

title: scikit-learn Usage
content:
Typical implementation:

from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression

model = LinearRegression(
    fit_intercept=True,
    positive=False
)

model.fit(X_train, y_train)

predictions = model.predict(X_test)

Important constructor parameters include fit_intercept, copy_X, n_jobs, positive, and
tol (for some implementations). After fitting, useful attributes include coef_,
intercept_, n_features_in_, and feature_names_in_.

title: Advantages and Limitations
content:
Advantages include simplicity, interpretability, fast training, and strong performance on
linearly related data. Limitations include sensitivity to outliers, inability to model
nonlinear relationships directly, susceptibility to multicollinearity, and reduced
performance when assumptions are heavily violated.

title: Linear Regression vs Ridge and Lasso
content:
Ridge Regression extends linear regression by adding L2 regularization, reducing coefficient
magnitude and improving stability when predictors are highly correlated. Lasso Regression
adds L1 regularization, encouraging sparse solutions by driving some coefficients exactly
to zero, which performs feature selection. ElasticNet combines both L1 and L2 penalties.
